en.extract:
The Somali ostrich (Struthio molybdophanes), also known as the blue-necked ostrich, is a large flightless bird native to the Horn of Africa. It is one of two living species of ostriches, the other being the common ostrich. It was also previously considered a subspecies of the common ostrich, but was identified as a distinct species in 2014. For a long time, Somalis partially domesticated some of these ostriches.


Taxonomy and systematics
Struthio molybdophanes was first described in the Norddeutsche allgemeine Zeitung Sunday Supplement of 16 September 1883 by Anton Reichenow, who noted the ostrich's distribution as extending over the plains of Somali- and western Galla-Land on the east coast of Africa from 10 degrees north to the Equator. Molecular evidence indicates that the East African Rift has served as a geographic barrier to isolate the taxon from the nominate subspecies, the North African ostrich S. c. camelus, while ecological and behavioural differences have kept it genetically distinct from the neighbouring Masai ostrich S. c. massaicus.  An examination of the mitochondrial DNA of Struthio taxa, including the extinct Arabian ostrich S. c. syriacus, has found that the Somali ostrich is phylogenetically the most distinct, appearing to have diverged from their common ancestor some 3.6 to 4.1 million years ago.


Description
Though generally similar to other ostriches, the skin of the neck and thighs of the Somali ostrich is blue-tinged grey (rather than pinkish), becoming brighter blue on the male during the mating season. The neck lacks any broad white ring. The wing primary and tail feathers are white in males, and brown in females. The males are larger than the females. The Somali ostrich is similar in size to other ostriches so far as is known, perhaps averaging marginally smaller in body mass than some subspecies of common ostrich (at least the nominate race, S. c. camelus). Reportedly Somali ostriches in captivity weigh about 105 kg (231 lb) but this may not be an accurate weight for wild birds as captive animals have feeding accesses not available to wild ostriches. It is thus one of the two largest extant bird species.


Distribution and habitat
The Somali ostrich is mostly found in the Horn of Africa, especially in north-eastern Ethiopia, southern Djibouti, most of Kenya, and across most of Somalia.


Behaviour and ecology
The Somali ostrich is differentiated ecologically from the common ostrich, with which there is some range overlap, by preferring bushier, more thickly vegetated areas, where it feeds largely by browsing, whereas the common ostrich is mainly a grazer on open savanna. There are also reports of interbreeding difficulties between the two taxa.


Relationship with humans

For a long time, Somalis partially domesticated some of these ostriches. People use their meats for consumption and the egg for ornamental use. Somali clans used to sell the feathers of tamed ostriches to coastal markets. 
The ostrich features prominently in Somali poetic and social language. The early 20th-century poet Qamaan Bulxan alluded to the feather's striking appearance in the line: "Baal-goray waa lagu ogaa, inuu bildhaamaaye!" ("It is known, that the ostrich's feather illuminates!").


Status and conservation
A report to the IUCN in 2006 suggests that the Somali ostrich was common in the central and southern regions of Somalia in the 1970s and 1980s.  However, following the political disintegration of that country and the lack of any effective wildlife conservation, its range and numbers there have since been shrinking as a result of uncontrolled hunting for meat, medicinal products and eggs, with the bird facing eradication in the Horn of Africa. In Kenya it is farmed for meat, feathers and eggs.


Notes


References

The '''Somali ostrich''' ('''''Struthio molybdophanes'''''), also known as the '''blue-necked ostrich''', is a large flightless bird native to the [[Horn of Africa]].<ref>{{cite book |last1=Redman |first1=N. |last2=Stevenson |first2=T. |last3=Fanshawe |first3=J. |title=Birds of the Horn of Africa: Ethiopia, Eritrea, Djibouti, Somalia, and Socotra - Revised and Expanded Edition |publisher=Princeton University Press |series=Princeton Field Guides |year=2016 |isbn=978-0-691-17289-7 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=QOs9DwAAQBAJ&pg=PA34 |access-date=2018-11-21 |page=34}}</ref> It is one of two living species of [[ostrich]]es, the other being the [[common ostrich]]. It was also previously considered a [[subspecies]] of the common ostrich, but was identified as a distinct species in 2014.<ref name="iucn" /> For a long time, Somalis partially domesticated some of these ostriches.<ref>Luigi Robecchi Bricchetti, ''Nell'Harrar'' (Milan: Fratelli Treves, 1896). [https://archive.org/details/nellharrar00bricgoog Available online].</ref>

== Taxonomy and systematics ==
''Struthio molybdophanes'' was [[Species description|first described]] in the ''[[Norddeutsche Allgemeine Zeitung|Norddeutsche allgemeine Zeitung]]'' Sunday Supplement of 16 September 1883 by [[Anton Reichenow]], who noted the ostrich's [[Species distribution|distribution]] as extending over the plains of [[Somalis|Somali]]- and western [[Oromo people#Origins and nomenclature|Galla]]-Land on the east coast of Africa from [[10th parallel north|10 degrees north]] to the [[Equator]].<ref>{{cite news |url=https://zefys.staatsbibliothek-berlin.de/kalender/auswahl/date/1883-9-16/28028685/ |title=Immer Neues aus Afrika |lang=de |author-link=Anton Reichenow |last1=Reichenow |first1=Anton |work=[[Norddeutsche Allgemeine Zeitung|Sonntags-Beilage zur Norddeutschen allgemeinen Zeitung]] |date=16 September 1883 |number=37 |page=148 [end of p. 14 in viewer]}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/32621301 |title=Immer Neues aus Afrika |lang=de |last1=Reichenow |first1=Anton |journal=Mittheilungen des Ornithologischen Vereines in Wien |date=October 1883 |volume=7 |number=10 |page=202}}</ref> Molecular evidence indicates that the [[East African Rift]] has served as a geographic barrier to isolate the taxon from the nominate subspecies, the [[North African ostrich]] ''S. c. camelus'', while ecological and behavioural differences have kept it genetically distinct from the neighbouring Masai ostrich ''S. c. massaicus''.<ref name="Freitag">{{cite journal |last1=Freitag |first1=Stephanie |last2=Robinson |first2=Terence J. |year=1993 |title=Phylogeographic patterns in mitochondrial DNA of the Ostrich (''Struthio camelus'') |journal=The Auk |volume=110 |issue=3 |pages=614–622 |url=http://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/auk/v110n03/p0614-p0622.pdf |doi=10.2307/4088425|jstor=4088425 }}</ref>  An examination of the [[mitochondrial DNA]] of ''Struthio'' taxa, including the extinct [[Arabian ostrich]] ''S. c. syriacus'', has found that the Somali ostrich is [[phylogenetics|phylogenetically]] the most distinct, appearing to have diverged from their common ancestor some 3.6 to 4.1 million years ago.<ref name="Freitag" /><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Robinson |first1=Terence J. |last2=Matthee |first2=Conrad A. |year=1999 |title=Molecular genetic relationships of the extinct ostrich, ''Struthio camelus syriacus'': consequences for ostrich introductions into Saudi Arabia |journal=Animal Conservation |volume=2 |issue=3 |pages=165–171 |doi=10.1111/j.1469-1795.1999.tb00062.x|bibcode=1999AnCon...2..165R }}</ref>

== Description ==
Though generally similar to other ostriches, the skin of the neck and thighs of the Somali ostrich is blue-tinged grey (rather than pinkish), becoming brighter blue on the male during the mating season.<ref name="Kenya">{{cite book |last1=Zimmerman |first1=Dale A. |last2=Pearson |first2=David J. |last3=Turner |first3=Donald A. |title=Birds of Kenya and Northern Tanzania |date=2001 |publisher=Christopher Helm |location=London |pages=78–79, 269 |edition=Revised reprint}}</ref> The neck lacks any broad white ring. The wing primary and tail feathers are white in males, and brown in females.<ref name="Kenya"/> The males are larger than the females.<ref name="OPS1" /><ref>{{cite book |title=Flightless birds |last1=Roots |first1=Clive |year=2006 |publisher=Greenwood Press |isbn=0-313-33545-1 |page=26}}</ref> The Somali ostrich is similar in size to other ostriches so far as is known, perhaps averaging marginally smaller in body mass than some subspecies of common ostrich (at least the nominate race, ''S. c. camelus''). Reportedly Somali ostriches in captivity weigh about {{convert|105|kg|lb|abbr=on}} but this may not be an accurate weight for wild birds as captive animals have feeding accesses not available to wild ostriches.<ref>{{cite thesis |last1=Davids |first1=A. H. |year=2011 |title=Estimation of genetic distances and heterosis in three ostrich (''Struthio camelus'') breeds for the improvement of productivity |degree=PhD |location=Stellenbosch |publisher=University of Stellenbosch}}</ref> It is thus one of the two largest extant bird species.

== Distribution and habitat ==
The Somali ostrich is mostly found in the [[Horn of Africa]], especially in north-eastern [[Ethiopia]], southern [[Djibouti]], most of [[Kenya]], and across most of [[Somalia]].<ref name="OPS1">{{cite book |title=Ostrich production systems, Part 1 |last1=Shanawany |first1=M.M. |last2=Dingle |first2=John |year=1999 |publisher=Food and Agriculture Organisation |isbn=978-92-5-104300-4 |page=12 }}</ref>

== Behaviour and ecology ==
The Somali ostrich is differentiated [[Ecology|ecologically]] from the common ostrich, with which there is some range overlap, by preferring bushier, more thickly vegetated areas, where it feeds largely by [[browsing (predation)|browsing]], whereas the common ostrich is mainly a [[grazing|grazer]] on open [[savanna]]. There are also reports of interbreeding difficulties between the two taxa.<ref name="Freitag" />

== Relationship with humans ==

[[File:Domestic ostriches at a Somali village in Bio Caboba, 1896. From Nell'Harrar by Luigi Robecchi Bricchetti.jpg|thumb|Domestic ostriches at a Somali village in Bio Caboba, 1888. From Nell'Harrar by Luigi Robecchi Bricchetti]]

For a long time, Somalis partially domesticated some of these ostriches.<ref>Luigi Robecchi Bricchetti, ''Nell'Harrar'' (Milan: Fratelli Treves, 1896). [https://archive.org/details/nellharrar00bricgoog Available online].</ref> People use their meats for consumption and the egg for ornamental use.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Somali Ostrich (''Struthio molybdophanes'') - BirdLife species factsheet |url=https://datazone.birdlife.org/species/factsheet/somali-ostrich-struthio-molybdophanes |website=BirdLife International Data Zone |access-date={{CURRENTDAY}} {{CURRENTMONTHNAME}} {{CURRENTYEAR}}}}</ref> Somali clans used to sell the feathers of tamed ostriches to coastal markets. <ref>{{cite book |last=James |first=Frank Linsly |title=The Unknown Horn of Africa: An Exploration From Berbera to the Leopard River |year=1888 |publisher=George Philip & Son |location=London |url=https://archive.org/details/unknownhornofafr00jame |access-date=2024-01-01}}</ref>

The ostrich features prominently in Somali poetic and social language. The early 20th-century poet Qamaan Bulxan alluded to the feather's striking appearance in the line: "Baal-goray waa lagu ogaa, inuu bildhaamaaye!" ("It is known, that the ostrich's feather illuminates!").<ref>{{cite book |last=Haybe |first=Ahmed Abdi |title=Qamaan Bulxan (Taarikh iyo Manso) |edition=Second |year=2001 |publisher=Scuola Tipografica Francescana |location=Harar |url=https://arcadia.sba.uniroma3.it/bitstream/2307/2571/1/Qamaan%20bulxan%20%28taariikh%20iyo%20maanso%29.pdf |access-date=2024-01-01 |language=so}}</ref>

== Status and conservation ==

A report to the [[International Union for Conservation of Nature|IUCN]] in 2006 suggests that the Somali ostrich was common in the central and southern regions of Somalia in the 1970s and 1980s.  However, following the [[Somali civil war|political disintegration of that country]] and the lack of any effective [[wildlife conservation]], its range and numbers there have since been shrinking as a result of uncontrolled [[hunting]] for meat, medicinal products and [[Ostrich egg|eggs]], with the bird facing eradication in the Horn of Africa.<ref>{{cite book |title=Wildlife trade in Somalia |last1=Amir |first1=Osman G. |year=2006 |publisher=World Conservation Union – Species Survival Commission |page=12 |url=http://www.wmenews.com/newsletters/File/Volume-1/Issue-4/support_art/Wildlife_Trade_in_Somalia_20061.pdf|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110718072734/http://www.wmenews.com/newsletters/File/Volume-1/Issue-4/support_art/Wildlife_Trade_in_Somalia_20061.pdf|url-status=usurped|archive-date=July 18, 2011}}</ref> In [[Kenya]] it is [[farmed]] for meat, feathers and eggs.

zh.extract:
灰颈鸵鸟（台湾作索馬利亞鴕鳥）（学名：Struthio molybdophanes），為野生鴕鳥中最高的，曾归类为鸵鸟的亚种之一，从2014年开始被视为独立的物种。


特徵
索馬利亞鴕鳥大致上外觀與鴕鳥類似，頭冠與北非鴕鳥相似，但有一寬闊白色頸環，頸部及腿部皮膚呈藍灰色。公鳥羽毛為黑到白。母鳥為灰色。


分布
索馬利亞鴕鳥主要分布在非洲之角，也有一些索馬利鴕鳥分布在索馬利亞、衣索比亞、肯亚等地区。
